pred_reason: SWC-101 applies due to missing access control allowing arbitrary configuration changes. SWC-107 applies because integer overflow/underflow risks exist throughout arithmetic operations in Solidity 0.4.x without protection. Evidence: setGiftTokenAmount and setGiftToken functions lack onlyOwner modifier, enabling unauthorized configuration changes; All arithmetic operations vulnerable to overflow/underflow due to Solidity 0.4.x version without SafeMath; Constructor and setPrices functions perform unsafe arithmetic calculations affecting core pricing logic

findings.Issue: Missing Access Control on Configuration Functions
findings.Description: The functions setGiftTokenAmount and setGiftToken lack the onlyOwner modifier, allowing any user to modify critical configuration parameters. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Unauthorized users can disrupt game mechanics, set malicious token addresses, or manipulate gift token amounts, potentially leading to economic disruption or fund loss.
findings.Location: Functions setGiftTokenAmount(uint256) and setGiftToken(address) at lines ~208 and ~215

findings.Description: The calculation costs[i] / 100 * fee in constructor and setPrices function performs division before multiplication, causing significant precision loss. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Incorrect fee calculations result in financial losses for the contract owner, as users receive more value than intended when characters are purchased.
findings.Location: Constructor and setPrices function; Static analysis shows 'divide-before-multiply' issues

findings.Description: Arithmetic operations lack overflow/underflow protection due to Solidity 0.4.x version. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential for incorrect state values, broken contract logic, and denial of service if calculations overflow or underflow, particularly in cost calculations and threshold settings.
findings.Location: Constructor arithmetic operations and setPrices function; All arithmetic operations throughout contract

findings.Issue: External Calls in Loop Without Protection
findings.Description: The hasEnoughTokensToPurchase function makes external ERC20 balanceOf calls within a loop without reentrancy protection. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Potential reentrancy attacks if malicious tokens are added, and possible denial of service if owner sets excessively long token arrays.
findings.Location: hasEnoughTokensToPurchase function; Static analysis shows 'calls-loop' low severity issue

findings.Description: Constructor and setPrices function use user-controlled array lengths without bounds checking. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Potential denial of service through gas exhaustion attacks if extremely long arrays are provided, affecting contract deployment and configuration updates.
findings.Location: Constructor and setPrices function; Static analysis shows 'controlled-array-length' high severity issues

findings.Description: Critical setter functions like setPercentageToKill lack input validation for logical constraints. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Owner can accidentally set invalid values (e.g., percentageToKill >= 100) that break core game mechanics and cause runtime errors.
findings.Location: setPercentageToKill function and other configuration setters
